blurb: A tall, willow-leaved native aster that erupts in sprays of white-to-pale-lilac daisies in fall — a vigorous colonizer for moist, sunny spots that needs room to roam.
water: Moderate; prefers moist soils but tolerates short dry spells
spread: Spreads vigorously by rhizomes — can form dense colonies; site and divide accordingly
seasons: Narrow lance-shaped leaves all season; sprays of 0.5–1 in. white to pale-lilac daisies (yellow centers) Sep–Oct; feathery seedheads persist into fall
wildlife: Important late-season nectar source for bumblebees, wasps, and hover flies; seedheads eaten by sparrows, finches, and grouse
deer: Moderately deer-resistant; tall, somewhat coarse foliage is not preferred

care.hardiness: Native to Colorado and broadly hardy through USDA zone 3 — no issue on the Front Range. Established colonies are tough and long-lived.[2]
care.planting: Set out transplants in spring (Apr–May) or early fall (Sep) in a site with reliable moisture — a rain garden, a low spot, a ditch edge, or a border that gets regular irrigation. Amend heavy clay with compost to improve root establishment, but this plant tolerates clay and even gravel once rooted. In containers, use a moisture-retentive potting mix and water frequently — the Front Range's dry air desiccates containers fast. Pot-grown panicled aster is much less winter-hardy in a raised container; sink pots into the ground or store in an unheated garage from November through March.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun gives the best bloom and sturdiest stems. Part shade (3–5 hrs) is tolerated, especially on the sunny Front Range where afternoon shade eases summer heat and keeps soil moister. Deep shade produces leggy, sparse-flowering plants.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable — loam, clay, gravel, or even rocky soil. Tolerates Front Range alkaline clay-loam well. This species naturally grows along streambanks, irrigation ditches, and moist prairie swales; give it a spot that doesn't dry out completely in midsummer.[1][2]
care.water: Moderate — provide consistent moisture, especially through midsummer heat. In Boulder's semi-arid climate, plan to water established plants every 7–10 days during dry spells. In a naturally moist site (drip line, rain garden, low bed), supplemental water may not be needed. It will survive drought but grows shorter and loses lower leaves.[1]
care.spacing: Space transplants 24–30 in. apart, knowing each plant can spread 2–4 ft wide via rhizomes within a few seasons. Best massed in a naturalistic or rain-garden planting with firm edging or a barrier to control spread into lawns or formal beds.[1]
care.propagation: By division: the easiest and most reliable method — divide the spreading rhizome clumps in early spring (March–April) every 2–3 years, replanting vigorous outer sections 24 in. apart. Division controls colony size and keeps plants blooming well. By seed: collect achenes when fluffy tufts are dry in fall; seeds generally germinate without stratification but a 4–6 week cold-moist chill improves rates. Sow on the soil surface in early spring; self-sown seedlings also appear near established plants.[1][2]
care.bloom: Late August through October on the Front Range — one of the latest native asters to peak. The branched, panicle-like sprays carry dozens of small white-to-pale-lilac daisies simultaneously, making a bold late-season display on a tall plant.[1][2]
care.feeding: Minimal to none. A light topdress of compost in spring supports growth on very poor soils, but avoid high-nitrogen fertilizer, which produces rank, floppy stems.[1]
care.maintenance: Cut stems down to 4–6 in. in early spring. Divide every 2–3 years to prevent the colony from overtaking adjacent plants — this species spreads assertively by rhizome in moist, fertile soil. Pinch stem tips once in early June if you want shorter, bushier plants; single stem-tip removal is enough. Stake tall plants in exposed, windy sites.[1]
care.selfsow: Also reseeds by wind-dispersed achenes. Seedlings are easy to identify and thin; the main management task is rhizome spread, not seedling overpopulation.[1]
care.troubles: Rust and powdery mildew can appear on lower leaves in dense plantings or humid conditions — thin plantings and ensure airflow. Aphids occasionally in spring; knock off with water. Root competition can suppress neighboring plants if the colony is left unchecked for several years.[1]
care.companions: Wild bergamot, tall goldenrod, Joe-Pye weed, blue wild indigo, red-twig dogwood — plants that tolerate or prefer moister sites and can hold their own against a colonizing aster.[1]

edible.summary: Panicled aster is not a food plant — it has documented Native American medicinal uses (wound dressings, salves, treating nosebleeds) but no edible use.[2][3]
edible.caution: Panicled aster (Symphyotrichum lanceolatum) has no recorded use as human food; its documented Indigenous uses were medicinal — the dried, powdered plant as a salve on abrasions, a decoction to dress wounds, and smoke from the crushed blossoms inhaled for nosebleeds — not culinary. Native asters are not known to seriously poison people or pets (the ASPCA lists asters as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses), so an accidental nibble is not an emergency, but the plant is not an accepted edible and should not be eaten. As with any daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant, people with ragweed/aster allergies may react to the pollen or foliage.[2][3]
